sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Back pain
History of Present Illness
The patient reports back pain that began approximately six days ago after walking 30-60 minutes per day. She describes the pain as a spasm that occurred suddenly during her walks, and it has persisted despite ibuprofen and heat application. She has been lifting more recently, approximately three times per week, and speculates it may have been related to a deadlift performed that day. There is no reported trauma. She denies numbness or weakness in her lower extremities. Her blood pressure readings at home have been well-controlled while dieting with her boyfriend. She reports some nasal congestion related to seasonal allergies.
Findings
Physical examination reveals mild tenderness to palpation on the right lateral aspect of the lumbar spine, with pain reproduced with flexion and extension. Straight leg raise is negative. The heart exam reveals a systolic ejection murmur, grade 2/6, which is stable. Vital signs are within normal limits. X-ray of the lumbar spine demonstrates normal bony alignment and no fracture. Laboratory results are unremarkable, with no elevated white blood cell count or signs of infection.
Assessment
Lumbar strain
Plan

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value: Prescribe meloxicam 15 mg daily. Advise ice and heat application. Refer to physical therapy for strengthening exercises. Continue lisinopril 10 mg daily. Order a refill of lisinopril. The nurse will schedule a follow-up appointment in four weeks if symptoms persist.
